subsystem: PHP Phar extension — phar_build(), the per-element callback used by Phar::buildFromIterator()/buildFromDirectory() to copy one iterator element (a string path, an open stream resource, or an SplFileInfo object) into a phar archive as a named entry.
provenance: Identified by the phar-specific symbols and types: struct _phar_t, phar_entry_data, phar_archive_object, phar_get_or_create_entry_data(), phar_entry_delref(), PHAR_MOD/PHAR_UFP, and the Zend/SPL API (zend_object_iterator, spl_ce_SplFileInfo, spl_filesystem_object, Z_TYPE_P, estrndup, add_assoc_str, php_stream_*). The header comment '/* sample: primevul_c_train_2425_vuln */' and the ZEND_HASH_APPLY_* return codes confirm this is ext/phar/phar_object.c from the PHP interpreter. The '/* {{{ */' fold marker is the PHP source style.
trust_boundary: The Iterator passed in is arbitrary PHP userland code: iter->funcs->get_current_data() and iter->funcs->get_current_key() (lines 18, 43, 177) return attacker/script-controlled values and keys, and the element value can be an arbitrary string path, resource, or SplFileInfo whose file_name/path/key are fully controlled. Every derived quantity — fname (the source path), str_key (the archive entry name), and str_key_len — is therefore hostile. The base directory (p_obj->b) is the caller-supplied prefix. Untrusted data crosses into filesystem operations (php_stream_open_wrapper, php_check_open_basedir) and into phar archive-entry creation (phar_get_or_create_entry_data with str_key as the internal member name).

what_the_code_does.explanation: The callback receives the live iterator and an opaque puser which is cast to struct _phar_t* — the accumulator carrying the base directory string b (base) with its length l (base_len), the class entry c (ce, for error messages), the destination phar wrapper p (phar_obj), the result array ret, and the destination stream fp. Note the mixed integer widths: str_key_len and base_len are uint32_t while fname_len and contents_len are size_t (64-bit on LP64). Several pointers (error, save, temp) are initialized to NULL so the cascade of cleanup 'if (save)/if (temp)/if (error)' guards later are well-defined; however value, fp, opened, str_key, str_key_len, data and contents_len are left uninitialized and must be assigned on every path that reads them.

The current element is pulled from the iterator by calling the userland-supplied get_current_data callback — the first crossing of the trust boundary. Because that callback is arbitrary PHP, it can throw; the code immediately checks EG(exception) and bails, and separately rejects a NULL value with an UnexpectedValueException. These two guards are the re-entrancy discipline for this call site: any object state touched after this point must still be valid after the callback ran.

The element type dispatches the three supported shapes. IS_STRING falls straight through (the string is treated as a source path, handled after the switch at line 129). IS_RESOURCE extracts the underlying php_stream via php_stream_from_zval_no_verify (a macro that sets fp), rejecting a NULL stream. This is the branch where the source is an already-open stream rather than a filename.

For a resource element the archive entry name must come from the iterator key. get_current_key is invoked (second userland callback), followed again by an EG(exception) check. The key must be a string; a non-string is rejected after zval_dtor releases it. Crucially, ZEND_SIZE_T_INT_OVFL(Z_STRLEN(key)) rejects a key whose size_t length would not fit in an int — this is the guard that makes the (int) cast on the next line safe against 32-bit truncation.

The key length is narrowed through (int) into the uint32_t str_key_len and a private NUL-terminated copy is made with estrndup, remembered in save for later efree. close_fp is cleared to 0 so the caller-owned resource stream is NOT closed by this function, and opened is set to a synthetic '[stream]' name. Control jumps to after_open_fp (line 236), deliberately skipping open_basedir and the filename-based stream open since fp already exists.

For an object element that is-a SplFileInfo, the code recovers the C-level spl_filesystem_object via the handlers->offset back-pointer arithmetic (the standard PHP embedded-object idiom). It requires a non-empty base directory for the object case, because the entry name for SplFileInfo elements is derived by stripping the base prefix from the resolved path (rather than from a key).

For a directory iterator entry, the full path is composed with spprintf from the container path plus DEFAULT_SLASH plus the directory entry name d_name. php_stat with FS_IS_DIR filters out actual directories (they are skipped with KEEP). Otherwise expand_filepath canonicalizes to an absolute path; the temporary composed fname is freed and replaced by the canonical test, whose ownership is tracked in save. Control jumps to phar_spl_fileinfo (line 132) where the base prefix will be stripped.

For SplFileInfo/SplFileObject the canonical path is taken from intern->file_name via expand_filepath, its length measured, ownership stored in save, and control jumps to phar_spl_fileinfo. Any object that is not an SplFileInfo, and any other zval type, falls through to the default case which throws and stops the iteration — so only the three intended element shapes proceed.

what_the_code_does.code:
fname = Z_STRVAL_P(value);
	fname_len = Z_STRLEN_P(value);
what_the_code_does.explanation: This is the tail of the IS_STRING branch (reached via the switch 'break'): the source path is taken directly from the element string. Note fname here aliases the zval's interned buffer (no copy, save stays NULL for this branch), so it must not be efree'd. Execution then falls into the phar_spl_fileinfo block below.

When a base directory is configured, it is canonicalized with expand_filepath into temp and adopted as base; base_len is recomputed from the resolved path via (int)strlen. The (int) cast here narrows a size_t into the local before it is re-widened into the uint32_t base_len, another place the code mixes signed/unsigned/32-bit widths for a length quantity.

The archive entry name is computed by removing the base directory from the front of the resolved path: str_key_len = fname_len - base_len and str_key = fname + base_len, then a leading slash/backslash is trimmed. The membership test is strstr(fname, base), which merely checks that base occurs SOMEWHERE within fname, not that it is a prefix; the subsequent pointer arithmetic nevertheless assumes prefix semantics. The guard 'str_key_len <= 0' is intended to reject the case where the path is not longer than the base.

If base is not found within fname the element is rejected with an exception and the two owned buffers (save, temp) are freed. This is the intended 'path is outside the base directory' rejection; its correctness depends entirely on the strstr test on line 146 truly meaning 'fname is under base'.

This is the else-branch of 'if (base_len)': with no base directory configured, the entry name is taken from the iterator key (string element case). It mirrors the resource branch exactly — userland get_current_key callback, EG(exception) check, string-type check, and the ZEND_SIZE_T_INT_OVFL guard so the following (int) cast cannot truncate.

The key is copied with estrndup into str_key (tracked by save) and str_key_len set. If the iterator provides no get_current_key at all, the element is rejected. After this block the entry name (str_key/str_key_len) and the source path (fname) are both established for the filename-based branches.

Before opening the source file, php_check_open_basedir enforces the PHP open_basedir sandbox on the attacker-influenced fname; a violation throws and cleans up save/temp. This is the security gate that constrains which host files a script can pull into the archive.

The source file is opened read/binary with STREAM_MUST_SEEK, and opened receives the resolved name string. Failure to open throws and frees save/temp. For the filename branches this is where fp becomes valid; close_fp stayed 1, so this fp will be closed by the function later.

The common tail (also the resource branch target). It refuses to add any entry whose name begins with the 5 bytes '.phar', because that name space is reserved for phar's internal magic directory (stub, signature, alias, metadata). The str_key_len>=5 sub-guard ensures the memcmp does not read past a shorter buffer. On skip it releases every owned resource (save, temp, opened, and fp only if close_fp).

The archive member is created (or fetched) by name using str_key/str_key_len as the internal entry path within the phar identified by fname/fname_len. On failure the error string is reported and freed, and all owned resources are released before stopping. This is the single call where the attacker-controlled entry name is committed into the archive's directory namespace; whatever normalization/traversal protection exists lives inside phar_get_or_create_entry_data (not shown).

On success the entry is retargeted to the 'user fp' representation (PHAR_UFP): any pre-existing modified stream is closed, offset is recorded as the current write position of the aggregate phar stream p_obj->fp, and the source fp is copied wholesale into p_obj->fp with php_stream_copy_to_stream_ex, which reports the number of bytes copied in contents_len. The filesizes are provisionally set from the position delta, then overwritten below.

Finalization: the source stream is closed only when this function owns it (close_fp — false for the resource branch). The resolved name string opened is stored into the result array p_obj->ret keyed by str_key (add_assoc_str copies the key and takes ownership of the opened zend_string). The owned buffers save/temp are freed, the entry's compressed/uncompressed sizes are set to contents_len, the entry reference is dropped with phar_entry_delref, and the callback returns KEEP to continue iterating.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: str_key is the archive entry name and is fully attacker-controlled: in the resource and no-base branches it is the raw iterator key (estrndup of Z_STRVAL(key), lines 62/196), and in the base branch it is the tail of a filesystem path the script chose (fname + base_len, line 157). The only name filtering performed in this function is the leading '.phar' skip (line 237). Nothing here rejects entry names containing '..', leading '/', or embedded path separators, so a hostile Iterator can dictate the internal member path written into the phar directory. Whether this yields a path-traversal / entry-spoofing primitive depends entirely on how phar_get_or_create_entry_data (implementation not provided) normalizes the name; the header-level contract is that this function hands it unsanitized, attacker-chosen bytes and a length. The implementation MUST canonicalize the name and reject traversal before committing the entry.

Two defects compound here. (1) strstr(fname, base) tests substring membership, not prefix: if the resolved base path happens to appear in the middle of fname (e.g. base '/tmp/x' occurring inside '/var/tmp/x/evil'), the test passes even though fname is NOT under base, defeating the intended 'inside the base directory' containment check while str_key = fname + base_len still slices the name at the wrong position. (2) str_key_len is uint32_t but fname_len is size_t, so 'str_key_len = fname_len - base_len' truncates to 32 bits, and the subsequent 'if (str_key_len <= 0)' compares an UNSIGNED value against 0 — the intended negative-length rejection is dead code (it only ever fires on exactly 0). Containment is guaranteed to keep base_len<=fname_len only when base is truly a substring, so the wrap is not currently reachable, but the guard's signedness and the width truncation are latent correctness/robustness hazards on any refactor. The implementation MUST use a real prefix comparison and a size_t signed-safe length check.

This is the sole validation gate protecting phar's reserved '.phar/...' magic namespace, and it is weak: it is a case-sensitive, exact-prefix, byte comparison. Depending on how the archive stores and later resolves member names, an attacker-supplied entry name that differs only in case ('.Phar', '.PHAR') or that reaches the magic directory by another spelling (leading separators, redundant './', backslashes on cross-platform archives) is not caught here and is passed on to phar_get_or_create_entry_data. If the resolver treats those variants as equivalent to '.phar', the skip is bypassed and a script could inject an entry into the reserved stub/signature/metadata space. The invariant is that the reserved-name test must match exactly the normalization the archive resolver uses.

get_current_data (line 18) and get_current_key (lines 43, 177) call back into arbitrary userland PHP in the middle of building an archive entry, holding raw C pointers (p_obj->fp, phar_obj->archive, ce). The code checks EG(exception) after each callback, but a malicious Iterator that does not throw yet mutates state during the callback — e.g. closing/destroying the Phar object, or reallocating the archive — could leave phar_obj->archive or p_obj->fp dangling when they are dereferenced at lines 258/291/293. The header-level risk is callback re-entrancy / object-lifetime (use-after-free class): the implementation MUST guarantee the phar object and its stream cannot be freed or re-entered for the duration of a single phar_build call (e.g. via an in-progress guard or by holding a reference).

what_could_go_wrong.code:
fname_len = spprintf(&fname, 0, "%s%c%s", test, DEFAULT_SLASH, intern->u.dir.entry.d_name);
						php_stat(fname, fname_len, FS_IS_DIR, &dummy);
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: For SPL_FS_DIR the source path is assembled from the container path and the directory entry name d_name and then used for php_stat and, after expand_filepath, for opening and archiving. d_name / the SplFileInfo path is attacker-influenced when the script drives a RecursiveDirectoryIterator over a location it controls (symlinks, crafted names). Combined with expand_filepath resolving symlinks, this is the point where the source-side of the copy can be steered to unexpected files; the open_basedir check on line 206 is the mitigating gate, so its correct application (and TOCTOU between php_stat/expand_filepath and the later open) is what must be confirmed. The invariant: the file resolved and stat'd here must be the same file opened at line 221, under the open_basedir policy.

summary: Application-level PHP phar archive-building callback whose entire input (source path fname and archive entry name str_key/str_key_len) originates from an arbitrary userland Iterator. There is no classic memory-corruption bug visible in the shown code, but the security posture rests entirely on weak or delegated validation gates: an attacker-controlled, essentially unsanitized entry name is handed to phar_get_or_create_entry_data (only a case-sensitive '.phar' prefix skip guards the reserved namespace), a substring (not prefix) base-directory containment test, unsigned/truncated length handling, and repeated userland callbacks that create object-lifetime exposure. The most important contracts to verify live in the unseen implementation.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Callback entry point; the security-relevant risk is captured under the callback re-entrancy finding, not the signature itself.
Opaque accumulator cast; fields (l, b, c, p, ret, fp) reviewed individually below.
32-bit length locals fed by size_t sources; truncation/signedness surfaces at lines 147-149.
contents_len is written by php_stream_copy_to_stream_ex before its use at line 312 on the only reaching path (else branch); fname_len is set on every branch. No uninitialized-use path.
Ownership tracked consistently by save/temp/error with NULL-guarded efree on every exit path; no double-free or leak found in the shown code.

Set on every path that reaches its uses (line 72 resource branch, line 221 file branch) and single-owned: released on skip/error paths, transferred to p_obj->ret via add_assoc_str at line 302. No UAF/double-free in shown code.
Userland callback across trust boundary; object-lifetime/re-entrancy contract.
Same callback re-entrancy exposure; folded into the get_current_data finding.
This is the guard that makes the following (int) cast safe against 32-bit truncation of the key length; it is a mitigation, not a defect.
Narrowing cast is bounded by the ZEND_SIZE_T_INT_OVFL guard immediately above; estrndup makes a bounded NUL-terminated copy.
NULL-checked immediately; extracts a caller-owned stream, close_fp cleared so it is not closed here.
Standard PHP embedded-object recovery after an instanceof check; not attacker-controllable arithmetic.
Source path built from attacker-influenced directory entry name; source-side path selection / TOCTOU exposure.
Canonicalization helper; its symlink resolution feeds the source-path finding but carries no independent buffer contract in the shown code.
Substring (not prefix) containment test undermines the base-directory boundary check.
size_t->uint32_t truncation and an unsigned '<= 0' guard that cannot catch negatives.
Pointer stays within the NUL-terminated fname because base_len<=fname_len is guaranteed by the substring match; covered by the strstr finding.
This is the mitigating sandbox gate; referenced by the source-path finding, not itself a defect.
NULL-checked open; potential TOCTOU relative to earlier stat is captured in the source-path finding.
Weak, case-sensitive, exact-prefix guard for the reserved magic namespace.
Commits an unsanitized, attacker-controlled entry name into the archive namespace.
Bulk copy with size reported into contents_len; no attacker-controlled length arithmetic in the shown code.
add_assoc_str copies the key and takes ownership of opened; single-owner transfer, no double-free with the subsequent save free.
Reference drop on the successfully created entry; refcount contract lives in the implementation but no misuse is visible here.

findings.title: Attacker-controlled phar entry name passed to entry creation without traversal/namespace sanitization
findings.bug_class: Improper input validation of archive member name (path traversal / entry-name spoofing)
findings.invariant: The internal phar member name (str_key/str_key_len) must be canonicalized and rejected if it escapes the archive namespace (contains '..', absolute/rooted components, or resolves into the reserved '.phar' directory) BEFORE the entry is created.
findings.failure_mode: A hostile Iterator supplies the entry name directly: in the resource and no-base branches str_key is the raw estrndup'd iterator key (lines 62/196); in the base branch it is fname+base_len (line 157). The only name filter in this function is the '.phar' prefix skip. If phar_get_or_create_entry_data does not normalize, a script can register members with crafted internal paths, corrupting the archive directory or shadowing reserved entries; on later extraction such names are a classic traversal sink.
findings.what_to_confirm: Inspect phar_get_or_create_entry_data (and phar_get_entry_info path normalization) to confirm it rejects '..', leading separators, and reserved-name spellings for the caller-supplied member name.

findings.title: Base-directory containment uses substring match, not prefix, plus truncating/unsigned length handling
findings.anchor: if (strstr(fname, base)) { str_key_len = fname_len - base_len; if (str_key_len <= 0) {
findings.bug_class: Incorrect containment check + numeric truncation / signedness of length
findings.invariant: The 'file is under base directory' test must be an anchored prefix comparison of canonical paths, and the derived entry length must be computed and compared in a width/signedness-safe way (size_t, guarded before subtraction).
findings.failure_mode: strstr(fname, base) returns true whenever base appears anywhere in fname, so a path that merely contains the base string but is not actually under it passes the boundary check, and str_key = fname+base_len then slices the entry name at the wrong offset producing a bogus member name. Separately, str_key_len is uint32_t while fname_len is size_t, so the subtraction truncates on paths near/over 4 GiB, and 'if (str_key_len <= 0)' on an unsigned value only ever catches exactly 0 — the intended negative-length rejection is dead. The substring guarantee keeps base_len<=fname_len today so the wrap is not currently reachable, but the checks are wrong-in-principle and fragile under refactor.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify whether callers ever supply a base that can appear non-prefix within fname, and whether a size_t/prefix-correct comparison is used upstream; confirm no length path can make base_len exceed fname_len.

findings.title: Weak reserved-namespace gate: case-sensitive exact-prefix '.phar' skip
findings.bug_class: Improper validation / inconsistent normalization of reserved name
findings.invariant: The test that skips entries destined for the reserved '.phar' magic directory must use exactly the same normalization (case folding, separator handling, leading-component stripping) that the archive resolver applies when it later interprets the member name.
findings.failure_mode: The skip only matches the literal, case-sensitive 5-byte prefix '.phar' at offset 0. If the archive resolver treats '.Phar', '.PHAR', or names reaching the magic directory via redundant separators/'./' or backslashes as equivalent to '.phar', those variants slip past this gate and are handed to phar_get_or_create_entry_data, allowing a script to inject into the reserved stub/signature/alias/metadata namespace. The memcmp itself is length-guarded so it does not over-read.
findings.what_to_confirm: Compare this prefix test against the phar name-normalization used at resolution time to confirm no case/separator variant of '.phar' bypasses the skip.

findings.title: Userland iterator callbacks fired mid-build create object-lifetime / re-entrancy exposure
findings.bug_class: Callback re-entrancy / use-after-free of phar object
findings.invariant: The phar object (phar_obj->archive) and destination stream (p_obj->fp) must remain valid and non-re-entrant for the whole duration of a phar_build call; a callback must not be able to free or re-enter them.
findings.failure_mode: get_current_data (line 18) and get_current_key (lines 43, 177) execute arbitrary PHP while phar_build holds raw pointers p_obj->fp, phar_obj->archive and ce. The code checks EG(exception) after each callback but not for silent state mutation: a malicious iterator that closes/frees the Phar or its stream during a callback without throwing leaves those pointers dangling when dereferenced at lines 258 (phar_obj->archive->fname), 291 and 293 (p_obj->fp), yielding a use-after-free. Real defense requires an in-progress guard or a held reference on the archive.
findings.what_to_confirm: Check whether Phar::buildFromIterator marks the archive in-progress / refcounts it so a userland callback cannot destroy phar_obj->archive or p_obj->fp mid-iteration.

findings.title: Source path built from attacker-influenced SplFileInfo/directory name (symlink & TOCTOU surface)
findings.anchor: fname_len = spprintf(&fname, 0, "%s%c%s", test, DEFAULT_SLASH, intern->u.dir.entry.d_name); php_stat(fname, fname_len, FS_IS_DIR, &dummy)
findings.code:
fname_len = spprintf(&fname, 0, "%s%c%s", test, DEFAULT_SLASH, intern->u.dir.entry.d_name);
						php_stat(fname, fname_len, FS_IS_DIR, &dummy);
findings.invariant: The file resolved/stat'd here (through expand_filepath symlink resolution) must be the same file the open_basedir check (line 206) approves and that php_stream_open_wrapper opens (line 221), with no window to swap the target.
findings.failure_mode: For a RecursiveDirectoryIterator the source path is composed from the container path plus the raw directory entry name d_name and canonicalized by expand_filepath, which follows symlinks. Because the script controls the traversed tree, it can point entries at symlinks or race the filesystem between php_stat/expand_filepath and the later open, potentially copying a file other than the one vetted by open_basedir. Impact is bounded by the open_basedir gate, so this is a robustness/defense-in-depth concern rather than a direct memory bug.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm open_basedir is enforced on the exact resolved path that is opened and that there is no exploitable stat-vs-open race for symlinked directory entries.

audit_checklist: In phar_get_or_create_entry_data: confirm the caller-supplied member name (str_key/str_key_len) is canonicalized and rejected for '..', leading/rooted separators, and reserved-name spellings before an entry is created.
Confirm the reserved-namespace skip on line 237 matches the archive resolver's actual normalization (case-insensitivity? separator/'./' handling?) so no variant of '.phar' bypasses it.
Replace the strstr(fname, base) containment test with an anchored canonical-prefix comparison and confirm no path can make base_len exceed fname_len before the line 147 subtraction.
Audit the uint32_t str_key_len / base_len vs size_t fname_len width mismatch; ensure lengths are handled as size_t with a signed-safe zero/negative check.

Verify Phar::buildFromIterator holds a reference / in-progress guard on phar_obj->archive and p_obj->fp so a userland get_current_data/get_current_key callback cannot free or re-enter them (UAF at lines 258/291/293).
Verify open_basedir (line 206) is enforced on the exact resolved path opened at line 221 and that there is no stat-vs-open TOCTOU for symlinked SPL_FS_DIR entries.
Confirm contents_len is always initialized on the only path that reads it (line 312 reached only through the else branch that calls php_stream_copy_to_stream_ex at line 293).
Confirm ownership discipline for opened (single owner, transferred by add_assoc_str at line 302; released on all skip/error paths) has no leak or double-release across the many early returns.
